standard_description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1–100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1–100 is prime or composite.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Find the prime factorization of the numbers below. Show your work.
200                    56                    91

Problem 1
a.   Take 60 seconds to list as many multiples of 2 as you can.
b.   What do you notice about the values in that list? What do you wonder?
c.   Is 3,498 a multiple of 2? How do you know?
Problem 2
a.   Make a list of the first fifteen multiples of 3.
b.   Which of the numbers in your list are multiples of 6? What pattern do you see in where the multiples of 6 appear in the list?
c.   Which numbers in the list are multiples of 7? Can you predict when multiples of 7 will appear in the list of multiples of 3? Explain your reasoning.

Complete each of the following:
a.   List all the factors of 48.
b.   List all the factors of 64.
c.   What are the common factors of 48 and 64?
d.   What is the greatest common factor of 48 and 64?

Problem 3
Tre has two alarm clocks set to go off in the morning. One alarm will buzz every 6 minutes, and the other will beep every 9 minutes. Both alarms go off at 6:15 a.m.
Tre thinks the next time that both alarms will go off at the same time will be in 54 minutes or at 7:09. Do you agree with Tre? Explain your reasoning.

Problem 1

Task
The 20 students in Mr. Wolf's 4th grade class are playing a game in a hallway that is lined with 20 lockers in a row.
###IMAGE0###
The first student starts with the first locker and goes down the hallway and opens all the lockers.
The second student starts with the second locker and goes down the hallway and shuts every other locker.
The third student stops at every third locker and opens the locker if it is closed or closes the locker if it is open.
The fourth student stops at every fourth locker and opens the locker if it is closed or closes the locker if it is open.
This process continues until all 20 students in the class have passed through the hallway.
Which lockers are still open at the end of the game? Explain your reasoning.
Which lockers were touched by only two students? Explain your reasoning.
Which lockers were touched by only three students? Explain your reasoning.
Which lockers were touched the most?

tasks.task_text:
4.OA The Locker Game
Task
The 20 students in Mr. Wolf's 4th grade class are playing a game in a hallway that is
lined with 20 lockers in a row.
The first student starts with the first locker and goes down the hallway and opens all
the lockers.
The second student starts with the second locker and goes down the hallway and
shuts every other locker.
The third student stops at every third locker and opens the locker if it is closed or
closes the locker if it is open.
The fourth student stops at every fourth locker and opens the locker if it is closed or
closes the locker if it is open.
This process continues until all 20 students in the class have passed through the
hallway.
a. Which lockers are still open at the end of the game? Explain your reasoning.
b. Which lockers were touched by only two students? Explain your reasoning.
c. Which lockers were touched by only three students? Explain your reasoning.
d. Which lockers were touched the most?
IM Commentary
Illustrative 
Mathematics

The purpose of this instructional task is for students to deepen their understanding of
factors and multiples of whole numbers. This is a classic mathematical puzzle; often it
is stated in terms of 100 lockers, so students have to make the connection to factors
and multiples to solve it. In this version, students can just go through all the rounds of
opening and closing locker doors and observe after the fact that there is a relationship
between the factors a locker number has and whether it is open or closed at the end.
This task provides students with an excellent opportunity to engage in MP7, Look for
and make use of structure (if they see early on that there is a relationship with factors
and multiples) or MP8, Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning (if they
start to see and describe the pattern as they imagine students opening and closing the
lockers).
Because the total number of lockers is only 20, students might answer the questions
without thinking about the underlying reasons for their answers. In the first question
for example, a student might say "1, 4, 9, 16 are all still open because I tried it out and
those were the ones that were left open." If a student goes this route, the teacher can
steer the conversation back to factors by asking the students if they notice anything
special about the numbers of the lockers which are still open. Asking students to try a
larger number of lockers (say 50 or 100) and repeating the game can also help students
look for a pattern since going through all the rounds of the game becomes less and
less feasible as the number of lockers increases. Once students see the pattern, they
should be pressed to explain why it works out this way.

items.question_content:
Kaley solved $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{2}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\ 
&=50 \times\maroonE{2}\\\\\  
&=100
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ | $=\redE{10}\times($ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]${}\times\maroonE{2})$  |
|$= \redE{10}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 3]]|
|$= 100$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Kaley solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=\blueE{50}\times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{10}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

Let's use the array to break the $7$ fours into two parts.

The dotted line breaks up the $7$ rows into $\blueD5$ rows and $\greenD2$ rows.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Above the line, there are $\blueD{5 \text{ rows of }4{ \text{ dots}}}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Below the line, there are $\greenD{2 \text{ rows of }4{ \text{ dots}}}$.

khan_exercises.title: Understand associative property of multiplication
khan_exercises.description: Practice changing the grouping of factors in multiplication problems to find equivalent expressions.

items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(9 \times 2) \times 5}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}=\greenD{9 \times (2 \times 5)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}=\maroonD{11 \times 5}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{11 \times 5} \\\\
\blueD{18 \times 5} &\neq  \maroonD{11 \times 5}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{11 \times 5}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(9 \times 2) \times 5}$:

* $\greenD{9 \times (2 \times 5)}$

* $\purpleC{9 \times 10}$

items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(9 \times 3) \times 3}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}=\greenD{9 \times (3 \times 3)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}=\maroonD{27 + 3}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{27 +  3} \\\\
\blueD{27 \times 3} &\neq  \maroonD{27 +3}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{27 +3}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(9 \times 3) \times 3}$:

* $\greenD{9 \times (3 \times 3)}$

* $\purpleC{9 \times 9}$

items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\purpleC{12 \times 5}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{12 \times 5} \\\\
\blueD{12 \times 5} &=  \purpleC{12 \times 5}
 \end{align}$

Yes, $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ is equivalent to $\purpleC{12 \times 5}$.

Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\maroonD{12 \times 10}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{12 \times  10} \\\\
\blueD{12 \times 5} &\neq  \maroonD{12 \times 10}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{12 \times 10}$.

Does $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}=\greenD{6 \times (2 \times 5)}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{(6 \times 2) \times 5}$:

* $\greenD{6 \times (2 \times 5)}$

* $\purpleC{12 \times 5}$

items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to ${5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} ?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} \\\\
\blueD{5 \times 10} &\neq  \maroonD{25 \times 10}
 \end{align}$

No, $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ is not equivalent to $\maroonD{(5 \times 5) \times (5 \times 2)} $.

Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\greenD{(5 \times 5) \times 2}?$

Yes! When multiplying, we can group numbers differently by moving the parentheses and still arrive at the same answer. [[☃ explanation 1]]

This is called the **associative property of multiplication**.

Does $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}=\purpleC{25 \times 2}?$

$
\begin{align}
\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{25 \times 2}\\\\
\blueD{(5 \times 5) \times 2} &\stackrel{?}{=}  \purpleC{25 \times 2}&&\text{Associative property of multiplication}\\\\
\blueD{25 \times 2} &=  \purpleC{25 \times 2}
 \end{align}$

Yes, $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$ is equivalent to $\purpleC{25 \times 2}$.

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{5 \times (5 \times 2)}$:

* $\greenD{(5 \times 5) \times 2}$

* $\purpleC{25 \times 2}$

The following expressions are equivalent to $\blueD{7 \times 3 \times3}$:

* $\maroonD{(7 \times 3) \times 3}$

* $\purpleC{7 \times 9}$

![The same dots in 3 groups of 5 groups of 2. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/713dc4059c8c803c8fd2088c10a3778912eb8f74)

There are $\blueD{3}$ groups of $\maroonD{5}$ groups of $\greenD{2}$.

We can change the grouping of the factors without changing the total.  

[[☃ explanation 1]]
$(\blueD{3} \times \maroonD{5})\times \greenD{2}= \blueD{3} \times (\maroonD{5} \times \greenD{2})$

DeShawn tried to evaluate $4\times82$ step by step. However, DeShawn made a mistake.


 | |$\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE{82}$
- | -
${\text{Step } 1}$ | $=$|$\purpleD4 \times (\maroonE{80}+\maroonE2)$ 
${\text{Step } 2}$ | $=$|$(\purpleD4 +\maroonE{80}) + (\purpleD4 +\maroonE{2}) $
${\text{Step } 3}$ |$=$|$84+6$
${\text{Step } 4}$ | $=$|$90$

**Find DeShawn's first mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
DeShawn showed a lot of strong math thinking. He tried to break $82$ up to make it easier to multiply.

Let's find where DeShawn changed the value of the expression so it was **not** equal to $\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82}$.

###Step 1

$\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82} \ {\large\overset{\checkmark}{=}} \ \purpleD4\times(\maroonE{80}+\maroonE2)$

###Step 2

$\purpleD4\times\maroonE{82}  \neq (\purpleD4 \redD+\maroonE{80}) + (\purpleD4 \redD+\maroonE{2}) $

DeShawn **incorrectly** put a $\redD{+}$ instead of $\times$ inside each set of parentheses.
DeShawn made a mistake in step $2$.

items.question_content:
**Which two expressions are equivalent to $42\text{ eights}$? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{42}\redE{\text{ eights}}$ is the same as $\purpleD{42}\times\redE{8}$ or $\redE{8} \times \purpleD{42}$.

Let's use the distributive property to find other ways to represent $\redE{8}\times \purpleD{42}$.

We can break apart $\purpleD{42}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleD{42}$.

$\maroonE8\times\purpleD{42} = \maroonE8\times (\purpleD{40} + \purpleD2)$

Next, we can distribute the $\maroonE8$ to each addend of $\purpleD{42}$.

$\maroonE8\times\purpleD{42}  = (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{40})+  \  (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{2})$

$42\text{ eights}$ is equivalent to the following expressions:

- $\maroonE8\times (\purpleD{40} + \purpleD2)$

- $(\maroonE8\times\purpleD{40})+ (\maroonE8\times\purpleD{2})$

We can distribute the $\maroonE5$ to each addend of $\purpleD{87}$.

$\purpleD{64}\maroonE{\text{ nines}}$ is the same as $\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE{9}$ or $\maroonE{9} \times \purpleD{64}$.

Let's use the distributive property to find other ways to represent $\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE{9}$.

We can break apart $\purpleD{64}$ into two numbers that add to $\purpleD{64}$.

$\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE9 = (\purpleD{60} + \purpleD4) \times\maroonE9$

We can distribute the $\maroonE9$ to each addend of $\purpleD{64}$.

$\purpleD{64}\times\maroonE9  = (\purpleD{60}\times\maroonE9)+  (\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE9)$

$64\text{ nines}$ is equivalent to the following expressions:

- $(\purpleD{60}\times\maroonE9)+ 
(\purpleD{4}\times\maroonE9)$

- $(\purpleD{60} + \purpleD4) \times\maroonE9$

items.question_content:
Music class has $7$ rows of desks.  There are $6$ desks in each row.

Amy multiplied $7\times6$ to find the total number of desks. 

Greg multiplied $6\times7$ to find the total number of desks. 

**Who is correct?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: Let's look at how Amy and Greg solve the problem.

###Amy's way

Amy multiplied $\blueD7 \times \purpleC6$ to find the total number of desks. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD7 \times \purpleC6$ is the same as $\blueD7$ groups of $\purpleC6$.

So, Amy is correct.

###Greg's way

Greg multiplied $\purpleC{6} \times \blueD{7}$ to find the total number of desks. 



[[☃ image 1]]

We can also group the array as $\purpleC6$ columns with $\blueD7$ dots each. 

The expression $\purpleC{6} \times \blueD{7}$ can also represents the array.

So, Greg is correct.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Both Amy and Greg are correct.
